full_response:
Let us convert the ordinary terrestrial acceleration  
 g = 9.81 m s⁻²  
into units of “light-years per year squared”.

1.  Convert g to metres / year²  

 (seconds in a year)  
 Δt = 1 yr = 365.2422 d × 86 400 s d⁻¹ = 3.15576 × 10⁷ s

 g = 9.81 m s⁻² × (3.15576 × 10⁷ s yr⁻¹)²  
   = 9.81 × 9.958 × 10¹⁴ m yr⁻²  
   = 9.77 × 10¹⁵ m yr⁻²

2.  Convert metres to light-years  

 (Length of 1 light-year)  
 1 ly = c Δt = 2.99792458 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 3.15576 × 10⁷ s  
    = 9.46073 × 10¹⁵ m

 Therefore  
 g = (9.77 × 10¹⁵ m yr⁻²) ÷ (9.46073 × 10¹⁵ m ly⁻¹)  
   ≃ 1.03 ly yr⁻²

The result differs from exactly 1 ly yr⁻² by only about 3 %, so for all practical purposes in this relativistic problem we may take

 g ≈ 1 light-year ⁄ year².

\boxed{g \simeq 1\;{\rm ly/yr^{2}}}

Confidence: 79%
